compiled_state.title: Outbound Bridge suffers excessive dispatch storms and push conflicts during merge sessions
compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body: Classification: behavioral, Score: 4 (INTERMEDIATE). Three root causes identified: (1) merge-to-main dispatches outbound bridge even when no ticket changes were pushed, (2) push step in workflow lacks pull-rebase retry causing non-fast-forward failures, (3) cancel-in-progress: false causes GitHub to silently cancel pending runs when queue fills.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body: Investigation complete. Three root causes: (1) merge-to-main.sh dispatches outbound bridge unconditionally even on no-op pushes, (2) shared concurrency group jira-bridge with cancel-in-progress:false causes queue storms, (3) workflow push step lacks pull-rebase-retry causing non-fast-forward failures. Confidence: high.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body:
## Fix 2 and Fix 3 are REJECTED — would introduce more severe bugs

### Fix 2 (pull-rebase-retry in workflow push step): REJECTED

Adding git rebase before push in the outbound bridge workflow would mask semantic conflicts and create silent data consistency issues.

**Problem 1 — Stale SYNC events committed on wrong base state**: When Run A pushes first and Run B rebases, Run B's SYNC events were computed against pre-Run-A state. The rebase makes the push succeed, but the SYNC events now sit on top of state they weren't computed against. If Run A synced ticket X's status to Jira and recorded a SYNC event, Run B (which also processed ticket X against the old state) would commit a SYNC event that may duplicate or contradict Run A's work.

**Problem 2 — Rebase on shallow clone is unreliable**: The workflow fetches tickets with --depth=2. Rebasing on a shallow clone fails when the common ancestor is outside the shallow history window. If origin/tickets advances by more than 2 commits between the initial fetch and the push attempt, the rebase fails with an opaque error.

**Problem 3 — The current push failure is correct safety behavior**: The non-fast-forward rejection correctly signals that the bridge ran against stale state and its output should be discarded. The right recovery is to re-run the bridge from scratch (which the next successful dispatch does), not to force the stale output onto a changed base.

**Problem 4 — Commit b992bd11 already tried blind rebase on main and replaced it**: The merge-to-main.sh history shows "fix: replace unconditional git pull --rebase with ancestor-aware merge" — this was a deliberate move AWAY from blind rebase because it caused problems. Introducing the same pattern in the workflow would repeat a known mistake.

**Problem 5 — Retry loop creates thundering herd**: A rebase-push-retry loop with multiple concurrent runs creates amplification where each retry conflicts with others' retries.

### Fix 3 (split concurrency groups + cancel-in-progress: true): REJECTED

The shared jira-bridge concurrency group with cancel-in-progress: false is a deliberate serialization mechanism. Splitting it would introduce race conditions that could cause data loss.

**Problem 1 — Inbound and outbound would race on tickets branch**: Currently impossible because they share a group. With separate groups, both could run simultaneously, both push to tickets, one fails. Worse: the outbound bridge might sync a ticket's local state to Jira while the inbound bridge is simultaneously pulling that ticket's Jira state back. The last writer wins, creating oscillation.

**Problem 2 — cancel-in-progress: true interrupts Jira API calls**: If outbound Run A is mid-way through syncing tickets to Jira (Jira API calls are not transactional), Run B arrives and cancels A. Run A made some Jira mutations but committed no SYNC events (killed before commit step). Run B processes the same events, re-syncs to Jira, creating duplicate comments, transitions, or field updates. The Jira side-effects from Run A are invisible — no SYNC events recorded, so they look like they never happened.

**Problem 3 — Multi-session parallel ticket operations**: Two agent sessions creating tickets simultaneously: Session 1 dispatches outbound Run A for tickets T1-T3, Session 2 dispatches Run B for T4-T5. With cancel-in-progress: true, Run B cancels Run A. T1 may be half-synced to Jira with no SYNC event. Run B processes T1-T5, re-syncing T1.

**Problem 4 — The cancellation storm is a symptom of Root Cause 1, not the concurrency group**: The 45/50 cancellations are GitHub's queue overflow behavior when bursts of dispatches arrive. Fix 1 (guard dispatch with SHA comparison) eliminates the bursts. With no bursts, the queue never overflows, and the serialization guarantee is preserved.

### Conclusion

Only Fix 1 (guard dispatch with SHA comparison) should be implemented. It addresses the root cause (unnecessary dispatches) without changing the serialization semantics that protect data consistency. Fix 4 (remove dead push trigger) is safe cleanup.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body:
## Run 23582249759 failure analysis

Timeline:
- 07:17:49 — Run created (workflow_dispatch)
- 07:19:18 — Fetched tickets branch (--depth=2)
- 07:19:21–07:29:31 — bridge-outbound.py ran (~10 min), produced 2 SYNC events
- 07:29:31 — Committed locally, push rejected (non-fast-forward)

Between 07:19 and 07:29, merge-to-main.sh runs pushed new commits to the tickets branch (the 07:27-07:29 burst of 15 dispatches confirms merge activity). The tickets branch advanced while bridge-outbound.py was running.

The concurrency group correctly serialized the next run (23582633068, created 07:29:27) which started at 07:29:41, fetched fresh tickets state, produced 2 SYNC events, and pushed successfully at 07:41:43.

Conclusion: The failure was correct safety behavior. The 2 SYNC events from the failed run were stale (computed against pre-push state). The successful follow-up run re-processed from current state. No data was lost.

Root cause: excessive dispatches from merge-to-main.sh (Root Cause 1). Fix 1 (SHA guard) will eliminate the burst, which eliminates the concurrent push that caused the conflict.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart
